body.creator: Ada
body.value: Alma says that she “finally understand[s]” Reynolds in this imagined future. They have been trying to understand each other since the beginning when Alma crashes into the table at the restaurant and catches Reynolds’s attention. This attempt to comprehend each other often takes the form of watching and wordless communication. On an allegorical level, too, the film depicts an attempt at intercultural understanding between the posh English Reynolds and the wilder, more energetic European Alma. What allows Alma's understanding to flourish, at least in her conception, is the poisoning, which breaks open what has been unstated in their relationship—namely, Reynolds’s desire for someone who can both dominate and care for him. It’s a twisted sort of romance, but one key feature of PTA’s filmmaking is his narratives’ and camera’s unwillingness to judge. Like Barry and Lena, like Alana and Gary, Reynolds and Alma represent two oddballs who find each other. Their actions, Alma’s use of poisoned mushrooms foremost among them, may be unpleasant to say the least, but PTA’s narrative gaze is not a judgmental one. It leaves those decisions to the audience and simply lets the characters live as they wish.
